segments.title: Inconstant's unarmoured iron hull and unusually long afterlife
segments.summary: Meet the iron-hulled screw frigate that entered service without armour, later became a training ship, and survived until the 1950s.
segments.body: Inconstant combined an iron hull with no armour, placing her at an important transitional point between wooden sailing frigates and later protected cruisers. The Royal Navy nevertheless regarded the substantial iron hull as meaningful protection even without an armour belt. After front-line service, the ship became a hulk and training vessel under the names Impregnable II and Defiance. Her survival from an 1868 launch until sale in 1955 was remarkable, but she reached the scrapyard before large-scale warship preservation had become established.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the unarmoured iron-hulled frigate and traces her training-ship afterlife to scrapping.

segments.title: The Wampanoag threat behind Inconstant's design
segments.summary: See why Edward Reed planned six fast frigates against an American design threat, then stopped after three when that threat faded.
segments.body: The Royal Navy conceived Inconstant as the first of six fast unarmoured frigates responding to the American Wampanoag class. Chief Constructor Edward Reed was answering a perceived threat from exceptionally fast American wooden frigates. Once the Wampanoag ships proved seriously flawed, the strategic pressure behind the British programme weakened. Inconstant and her sisters were also expensive, helping cut the intended six-ship class to only three vessels.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Reed's six-ship plan, its American stimulus, and why only three were built.

segments.title: Eleven boilers, a giant propeller, and 16 knots
segments.summary: Explore the machinery and dimensions that made Inconstant a fast, long-legged steam-and-sail frigate for her era.
segments.body: Eleven boilers fed a single two-cylinder trunk engine driving a 23-foot propeller, producing 7,360 indicated horsepower. That machinery moved a ship roughly 337 feet long and nearly 5,900 tons displacement at a claimed 16-knot top speed. Her stated steaming range was 2,780 nautical miles at 10 knots, a figure Dr. Clarke treats as an aggregate drawn from several sources. The discussion distinguishes this coal-limited steaming radius from the much less constrained endurance available under her full ship rig.

evidence.note: The transcript gives the ship's machinery, dimensions, horsepower, speed, rig, and stated steaming range.

segments.title: Inconstant's 9-inch and 7-inch rifled muzzle-loaders
segments.summary: Compare the ammunition and useful fighting ranges of Inconstant's heavy rifled muzzle-loading broadside guns.
segments.body: Inconstant carried ten 9-inch and six 7-inch rifled muzzle-loaders. The 7-inch weapons could fire Palliser armour-piercing shells as well as common and shrapnel ammunition weighing roughly 112 to 115 pounds. Surviving 9-inch guns at Simon's Town and Hurst Castle help connect the ship's battery to preserved examples. Dr. Clarke stresses the practical difference between advertised maximum range and effective range, placing useful reach at roughly 9,000 yards for the 9-inch gun and 5,000 for the 7-inch.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the gun battery, ammunition, surviving examples, and effective ranges.

segments.title: Armour-piercing guns on an unarmoured ship
segments.summary: The ship's broadside could threaten iron armour even though Inconstant herself carried no armour protection.
segments.body: The main-deck 9-inch guns sat in a traditional broadside and were credited with penetrating 11.3 inches of wrought iron. The upper-deck battery included two forward chase guns, and its 7-inch weapons could reportedly pierce 7.7 inches. Those figures gave Inconstant serious offensive power against armoured targets. They also expose the design's central tactical contradiction: armour-piercing guns were mounted in a hull unable to absorb comparable punishment.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts the guns' stated wrought-iron penetration with the ship's lack of armour.

segments.title: Channel Squadron service followed by eight years in reserve
segments.summary: Follow Inconstant from Pembroke construction and a prestigious Channel posting into the Victorian reserve system.
segments.body: Built at Pembroke Dockyard and fitted out at Portsmouth, Inconstant commissioned in 1869 for the prestigious Channel Squadron. She later joined Rear Admiral Frederick Seymour's Detached Squadron for visits to Scandinavian ports. Paid off in 1872, she then spent eight of her first twelve years in reserve before recommissioning. That pattern illustrates how the Victorian Royal Navy preserved maintained hulls and reduced peacetime operating costs while retaining the ability to expand in war.

evidence.note: The transcript covers construction, commissioning, early squadron duty, payoff, and the logic of reserve service.

segments.title: A flagship for Seymour and Victorian gunboat diplomacy
segments.summary: See why a senior admiral recalled Inconstant from reserve and used her to lead a globe-circling Detached Squadron.
segments.body: Frederick Seymour remembered Inconstant from his earlier command and requested her as his Mediterranean Fleet flagship when she returned from reserve in 1880. Dr. Clarke presents that choice by an experienced admiral holding the Royal Navy's most prestigious sea command as strong evidence that the ship was genuinely useful. Inconstant then served as a Detached Squadron flagship under Richard Meade and, after Meade became ill, Francis Sullivan. The squadron joined global cruising to coercive naval diplomacy, using a visible concentration of firepower to reinforce British demands abroad.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Seymour's choice and the Detached Squadron's diplomatic purpose and world cruise.

segments.title: World cruise, shipboard fire, and the Anglo-Egyptian War
segments.summary: Trace Inconstant's eventful circumnavigation through a royal passenger, a fire at sea, and operations in Egypt.
segments.body: The 1880-82 circumnavigation carried the future George V between Melbourne and Sydney and included a reported sighting of a supposed phantom ship. Inconstant also diverted toward Simon's Town for possible service in the First Boer War, although hostilities ended before she arrived. On the return voyage, the crew stopped a serious fire by flooding the after compartments, illustrating the practical demands of damage control at sea. The squadron then diverted to Egypt, where Inconstant participated in the Anglo-Egyptian War and landed sailors for operations ashore.

evidence.note: The transcript recounts the voyage, fire response, Egyptian diversion, bombardment context, and landing party.

segments.title: From fighting ship to accommodation and training hulk
segments.summary: Follow the old frigate's later transformation into accommodation, gunnery, boys', and torpedo-training roles.
segments.body: After returning to reserve in 1882, Inconstant eventually became accommodation for overflow from the Devonport barracks. She next served as a gunnery training ship and as part of the Impregnable boys' training establishment before transferring to the Defiance torpedo school. These roles explain the repeated name changes that can obscure the old frigate's identity in later records. Her long reuse shows how a hull could remain institutionally valuable long after it ceased to be a credible front-line cruiser, and she was still the second-to-last Welsh-built naval vessel afloat when broken up.

evidence.note: The transcript traces reserve, accommodation, gunnery, boys' training, torpedo training, and final scrapping.

segments.title: A fast sailing frigate with retractable steam machinery
segments.summary: Learn how Inconstant reduced drag under sail and protected her iron hull for long ocean deployments.
segments.body: Inconstant reportedly reached 13.5 knots under sail and was counted among the fastest warships of her day. Her propeller could be hoisted into the hull and her funnels lowered, reducing drag when the full ship rig supplied propulsion. The iron hull was covered by two three-inch layers of oak and then copper sheathing to limit biofouling during long deployments. Dr. Clarke also suggests that this layered construction contributed to her reputation as a good seaboat and steady gun platform.

evidence.note: The transcript describes sailing speed, the hoistable propeller, lowered funnels, oak backing, and copper covering.

segments.title: Why Inconstant made sense on distant ocean stations
segments.summary: The ship's speed and ocean mobility were useful abroad, where she was less likely to meet newer protected or armoured cruisers.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats Inconstant's repeated selection as a flagship as practical proof of usefulness rather than merely paper performance. Her speed, sailing endurance, and good behaviour at sea suited a Detached Squadron expected to range around the world. Sending an unarmoured cruiser into open oceans and distant stations also reduced the chance that she would face protected and armoured opponents she could not safely fight. In that environment, room to manoeuvre let the Royal Navy exploit mobility and presence while limiting the design's protection weakness.

evidence.note: The transcript links Seymour's confidence and Detached Squadron deployment to the ship's strengths and protection limits.

segments.title: Comparing Inconstant, Raleigh, and Shah
segments.summary: Compare the three related frigates by dockyard, machinery supplier, construction date, cost, and armament.
segments.body: The class comparison shows that Inconstant, Raleigh, and Shah came from Pembroke, Chatham, and Portsmouth Royal Dockyards respectively. Each used machinery from a different supplier, and the later two ships were laid down several years after Inconstant. Brassey's 1887 figures put their costs excluding armament at roughly £213,000, £193,000, and £235,000, showing substantial variation within the related group. Shah also carried a revised battery of two 9-inch, sixteen 7-inch, and eight 5-inch guns.

evidence.note: The transcript compares the three ships' builders, engines, dates, costs, and Shah's armament.

segments.title: HMS Shah at the Battle of Pacocha
segments.summary: Watch two unarmoured British ships struggle to contain the turret ship Huáscar in shallow water and launch the first torpedo fired in anger.
segments.body: As Pacific Squadron flagship, Shah fought the Peruvian turret ship Huáscar at the Battle of Pacocha alongside the corvette Amethyst. The two unarmoured British ships had to keep clear of Huáscar's turret guns because a damaging hit could be disastrous. Although Shah could make about 16 knots, the smaller Peruvian ship used the shallow water to manoeuvre more freely. Shah fired the first self-propelled torpedo used in combat, but it missed, and the action exposed the tactical limits of speed and firepower without protection.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the Battle of Pacocha, Huáscar's shallow-water advantage, and Shah's missed torpedo.

segments.title: Shah's soldiers, naval guns ashore, and Bermuda afterlife
segments.summary: Follow Shah from troop transport and naval-brigade support in southern Africa to reserve and accommodation service in Bermuda.
segments.body: On her homeward voyage in 1879, Shah received news of the British defeat at Isandlwana and diverted to carry troops to Durban. She contributed sailors and heavy naval guns to the Anglo-Zulu War, where naval weapons supported mobile firing trains ashore. After returning to Britain and entering the steam reserve, she transferred to the North America and West Indies Station. At Bermuda she provided dockyard accommodation from 1897 and later became a coal-storage hulk before sale in 1919.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Shah's South African diversion, naval contingent, reserve status, Bermuda accommodation role, and coal hulk conversion.

segments.title: How pieces of Shah survived after the ship
segments.summary: Shah disappeared as a hull, but her iron masts, stern plaque, and crew monument preserved parts of her story.
segments.body: Shah's iron masts were judged a lighter and more durable replacement for Victory's wooden masts. They were fitted when Victory entered dry dock in 1887 and survived into the present, giving part of the lost cruiser an unusually prominent afterlife. Shah's stern plaque, a gift associated with the Persian ruler for whom she was named, survives in Bermuda. A Portsmouth monument also commemorates her crew, spreading the ship's material legacy across several sites even though the hull itself disappeared.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the surviving masts, plaque, and crew monument.

segments.title: The industrial dilemma behind building a minor navy
segments.summary: A closing design exercise asks whether a smaller South American state should buy ships abroad or first build the industry needed to sustain a navy.
segments.body: The unanswered exercise places the viewer in charge of a smaller South American state in the 1870s or 1880s, outside the region's major naval powers. The first choice is whether to build a navy at all and, if so, what force structure the state can sustain. Buying warships abroad offers a faster route to capability, while domestic construction promises greater autonomy but requires long-term investment. The prompt therefore frames naval power as an industrial question: iron, suppliers, skilled labour, and infrastructure may be harder to acquire than the warships themselves.

evidence.note: The transcript poses an audience exercise about naval force design and industrial development for a minor South American state.
